original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.0005, what caveat is documented for SNMP?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: SNMP - When SNMP is enabled via the switch CLI, it can take between 1-2 minutes for the SNMP daemon to be ready to respond to requests. If a local or external SNMP MIB walk is performed in the interval between when SNMP is first enabled and the SNMP daemon is ready, the MIB walk action will return an error.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.0005, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.0005, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.0005, what issue was resolved in WebUI Bug 282016?
original_answer: WebUI (Bug ID 282016): (For 6300 Switch Series only) Symptom: The Interface Edit button on WebUI PoE interface page is disabled when the selected PoE interface's power enable status setting is disabled. Scenario: This issue occurs when a user accesses the WebUI's PoE Interface page on a PoE switch, edits the PoE configuration of an interface/port, sets the Power Enable option to disabled, then saves these settings. After this, the Edit button for this interface/port on WebUI will not be enabled to reconfigure this interface/port for any other PoE configuration. Workaround: Edit the PoE configuration of Interface/Port from the command-line interface or re-enable the Power Enable option for the Interface/Port from the CLI to reenable the Edit button again in the WebUI.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.0005, what issue was resolved in Activate Bug 279732?
original_answer: Activate (Bug ID 279732): Symptom: The switch's connection to Aruba Central fails. Scenario: When disabling and then reenabling the aruba-central location-override setting on the switch, a missing Activate server URL causes the device connection to Central to fail. Workaround: Connect the switch to the Central cluster directly using the central location-override command in the switch command-line interface. Alternatively, remove the central location-override configuration from the switch, ensure that aruba-central is enabled on the switch, and then reboot that device.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.0005, what issue was resolved in WebUI Bug 279019?
original_answer: WebUI (Bug ID 279019): Symptom: After uploading an invalid PEM certificate, the hpe-restd process becomes unstable, the WebUI temporarily stops responding, and all REST API calls from the WebUI fail. Scenario: If a user uploads a corrupt PEM certificate file using WebUI certificate management window, selecting the Upload button in the WebUI causes the WebUI to stop working completely. To recover from this state, restart hpe-restd from the bash prompt in the command-line interface or restart the switch. Workaround: Use the CLI to upload certificates.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.0005, what issue was resolved in Internal srvcs: Security PA infra Bug 278954?
original_answer: Internal srvcs: Security PA infra (Bug ID 278954): Symptom: Clients are onboarding with an incorrect auth-priority order. Users onboarded with a lower-priority authentication method, so higher-priority authentication requests were not seen. Scenario: When concurrent onboarding is enabled with a default authentication priority and non-default auth-precedence on a port, clients onboarding are taking the wrong auth-priority. Workaround: At the issue state, reconfigure the auth-priority on the port to onboard the clients.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.0005, what issue was resolved in TPM Bug 265125?
original_answer: TPM (Bug ID 265125): Symptom: Failure to establish or maintain a connection to Aruba Central. Scenario: A switch may fail to establish or maintain its relationship with Aruba Central. In these cases, the switch may display the "Central source connection status" field in the "show aruba-central" command will read "connection_failure". Workaround: Issue the following commands in order: start-shell; sudo bash; systemctl restart_jitterentropy-rngd; exit; exit; show aruba-central.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.0005, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.0005, how do you restore configuration after a software downgrade?
original_answer: Downgrade Restore: To restore configuration after a software downgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, PL.10.xx.yyyy). 2. Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. 3. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.0005, how do you perform the software upgrade?
original_answer: Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method. 2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue. 3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow unsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time. 4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.
cleaned_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.0005, how do you perform the software upgrade?
cleaned_answer:
Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow these steps in order:
1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.
2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any device updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue.
3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow unsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.0010, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.0010, what known issue is documented for Device Profile Bug 288885?
original_answer: Device Profile (Bug ID 288885): Symptom: On a dual port AP, a device profile client failed to onboard on a LAG interface with secure mode enabled. This issue is seen on a device profile with lldp-group matching criteria, but is not observed on a device profile with mac-match matching criteria. Scenrio: This issue occurs when a user connects the Dual AP on some normal physical ports (for example, 1/1/1 and 1/1/2) and makes sure these two ports NOT part of the LAG. LLDP frames exchanged and the output of the show lldp neighbors command shows the entries. Next, configure LAG with AAA and port-access commands to enable security. Add these twp ports to the LAG interface and then observe the Dual AP onboard failure by issuing the command show port-access clients interface lag<lag id>. Workaround: Clear LLDPneighbors and toggle the LAG interface on DUT.

Scenario: This issue occurs when a user connects the Dual AP on some normal physical ports (for example, 1/1/1 and 1/1/2) and makes sure these two ports are NOT part of the LAG. LLDP frames exchanged and the output of the show lldp neighbors command shows the entries. Next, configure LAG with AAA and port-access commands to enable security. Add these two ports to the LAG interface and then observe the Dual AP onboard failure by issuing the command show port-access clients interface lag<lag id>. Workaround: Clear LLDPneighbors and toggle the LAG interface on DUT.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.0010, how do you restore configuration after a software downgrade?
original_answer: Downgrade Restore: To restore configuration after a software downgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, PL.10.xx.yyyy). 2. Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. 3. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.0010, how do you perform the software upgrade?
original_answer: Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method. 2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue. 3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow unsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time. 4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.
cleaned_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.0010, how do you perform the software upgrade?
cleaned_answer:
Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow these steps in order:
1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.
2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any device updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue.
3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow unsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1000, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1000, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1000, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1000, how do you restore configuration after a software downgrade?
original_answer: Downgrade Restore: To restore configuration after a software downgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, PL.10.xx.yyyy). 2. Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. 3. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1000, how do you perform the software upgrade?
original_answer: Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method. 2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue. 3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow unsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time. 4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.
cleaned_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1000, how do you perform the software upgrade?
cleaned_answer:
Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow these steps in order:
1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.
2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any device updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue.
3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow unsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1001, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.
cleaned_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1001, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?

Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1001, how do you restore configuration after a software downgrade?
original_answer: Downgrade Restore: To restore configuration after a software downgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, PL.10.xx.yyyy). 2. Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. 3. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1001, how do you perform the software upgrade?
original_answer: Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method. 2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue. 3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow unsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time. 4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.
cleaned_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1001, how do you perform the software upgrade?
cleaned_answer:
Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow these steps in order:
1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.
2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any device updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue.
3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow unsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1005, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1005, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1005, what caveat is documented for SNMP?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: SNMP - When SNMP is enabled via the switch CLI, it can take between 1-2 minutes for the SNMP daemon to be ready to respond to requests. If a local or external SNMP MIB walk is performed in the interval between when SNMP is first enabled and the SNMP daemon is ready, the MIB walk action will return an error.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1005, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1005, how do you restore configuration after a software downgrade?
original_answer: Downgrade Restore: To restore configuration after a software downgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, PL.10.xx.yyyy). 2. Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. 3. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1005, how do you perform the software upgrade?
original_answer: Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method. 2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue. 3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow unsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time. 4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.
cleaned_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1005, how do you perform the software upgrade?
cleaned_answer:
Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow these steps in order:
1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.
2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any device updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue.
3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow unsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1010, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1010, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1010, what issue was resolved in VSX Bug 289568?
original_answer: VSX (Bug ID 289568): Symptom: The root switch initiates STP re-convergence and causes causing MAC flush and traffic loss. Scenario: This issue occurs during an upgrade process in VSX pair (which acts as non-root switch in RPVST topology). When the primary switch is down, this issue is encountered in a non-root access switch connected to this VSX-pair. Workaround: Install BPDU filter on the root-port of access switch during the software upgrade and remove the filter after the upgrade.
cleaned_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1010, what issue was resolved in VSX Bug 289568?

Scenario: This issue occurs during an upgrade process in a VSX pair acting as non-root switch in RPVST topology. original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1010, what issue was resolved in Central Bug 293314?
original_answer: Central (Bug ID 293314): Symptom: The client list is not populated in the Central client view page. Scenario: In very rare instances, after maintenance is performed on Aruba Central infrastructure, the client list in the Central client view page may fail to list the connected clients for some switches. This issue is uncommon enough that an upgrade to this release may not be warranted if the only reason for the upgrade is to avoid this potential issue. Workaround: From the switch console, enter bash via the start-shell command, and execute the command systemctl restart yang-resolverd.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1010, what issue was resolved in Port Access Bug 297755?
original_answer: Port Access (Bug ID 297755): Symptom: Traffic to existing clients on a VLAN drops if a new client with the MAC address of a DUT system onboards an authentication enabled port. Scenario: This issue is observed when a packet with a MAC address which is same as the MAC address of a DUT system is received on the authentication enabled port. Workaround: Shut down and re-enable the VLAN interface to resume traffic flow for the clients.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1010, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1010, how do you restore configuration after a software downgrade?
original_answer: Downgrade Restore: To restore configuration after a software downgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, PL.10.xx.yyyy). 2. Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. 3. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1010, how do you perform the software upgrade?
original_answer: Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method. 2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue. 3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow unsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time. 4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.
cleaned_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1010, how do you perform the software upgrade?
cleaned_answer:
Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow these steps in order:
1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.
2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any device updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue.
3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow unsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1020, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1020, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_answer: Feature Caveat: Certificates - When a switch uses a certificate with a legacy certificate name that is not supported in 10.12 because it contains disallowed characters, the information will migrate properly in the upgrade, but that certificate can no longer be edited. original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1020, what issue was resolved in NTP Bug 306746?
original_answer: NTP (Bug ID 306746): Symptom: The last_configuration_time is not updated on the switch. Workaround: If the NTP configuration must be updated via CLI, then toggle the management interface from On to Off and then toggle it back to On.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1020, what issue was resolved in Port Access Bug 301734?
original_answer: Port Access (Bug ID 301734): Symptom: The role assigned by CoA gets overridden by low-priority RADIUS assigned roles. Scenario: With default auth-priority set on a port, configure both MAC and 802.1X authentication. After a successful MAC authentication and concurrent onboarding, assign a role from the RADIUS server. While 802.1x is still in the authenticating state, assign a different RADIUS role using CoA and wait till the 802.1X gets deauthenticated. It can be observed that the role assigned by CoA gets overridden by the RADIUS assigned role. Workaround: Remove concurrent onboarding.

reason: The original answer contains irrelevant information about a firmware upgrade and does not directly address the issue resolved in ACL Logging Bug 305843.
original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1020, what issue was resolved in ACL Logging Bug 305843?
original_answer: ACL Logging (Bug ID 305843): Symptom: Switch reloads unexpectedly during a firmware upgrade. Scenario: This issue occurs during an upgrade where a switch might reload while parsing an Access List (ACL) configuration. Workaround: It is recommended to follow the following procedure before the upgrade: Save the full configuration as a checkpoint using the copy running-config checkpoint <name> command. Remove all access-list configurations, save the configuration as the startup configuration using the copy running-config startup-config command. Upgrade the switch. Once the switch is upgraded, move the ACLs back to the running configuration by copying the saved checkpoint to the running configuration using the copy checkpoint <name> running-config command.

Remove all access-list configurations, save the configuration as the startup configuration using the copy running-config startup-config command. Upgrade the switch. Once the switch is upgraded, move the ACLs back to the running configuration by copying the saved checkpoint to the running configuration using the copy checkpoint <name> running-config command.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1020, what is the workaround for ACL Logging Bug 305843?
original_answer: ACL Logging (Bug ID 305843): The documented workaround is: It is recommended to follow the following procedure before the upgrade: Save the full configuration as a checkpoint using the copy running-config checkpoint <name> command. Remove all access-list configurations, save the configuration as the startup configuration using the copy running-config startup-config command. Upgrade the switch. Once the switch is upgraded, move the ACLs back to the running configuration by copying the saved checkpoint to the running configuration using the copy checkpoint <name> running-config command.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1020, what issue was resolved in Supportability Bug 311012?
original_answer: Supportability (Bug ID 311012): Symptom: High storage utilization is observed in the file system and as a result, users are unable to copy the support files. Scenario: Logs from various daemons are continuously generated and stored in multiple files in the /fs/logs/boot/ directory. This leads to a high storage utilization and prevents the copying of support files. Workaround: Use the directory mode support for file collection. After upgrading the device to the fixed firmware version, it is necessary to remove the previous boot logs. Performing two system reboots will clear these files.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1020, what issue was resolved in RADIUS Bug 308854?
original_answer: RADIUS (Bug ID 308854): Symptom: Client authentication fails and packets sent to the RADIUS server are timed out by the switch. Scenario: This issue is observed when the connection to the RadSec server is lost and restored when multiple port-access users are authenticated to the RADIUS server. As a result, the switch might incorrectly time out the RADIUS packets. Workaround: Remove and re-configure the RadSec server.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1020, how do you restore configuration after a software downgrade?
original_answer: Downgrade Restore: To restore configuration after a software downgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, PL.10.xx.yyyy). 2. Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. 3. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1020, how do you perform the software upgrade?
original_answer: Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method. 2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue. 3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow unsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time. 4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.
cleaned_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1020, how do you perform the software upgrade?
cleaned_answer:
Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow these steps in order:
1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.
2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any device updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue.
3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow unsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1030, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1030, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1030, what caveat is documented for Hot Patch?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Hot Patch - When a hot-patch file download is triggered using the switch WebUI, log messages can incorrectly state that the file is added to the database with a missing status. This is a temporary state, and will correctly change to Not applied once the download is completed.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1030, what caveat is documented for SNMP?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: SNMP - When SNMP is enabled via the switch CLI, it can take between 1-2 minutes for the SNMP daemon to be ready to respond to requests. If a local or external SNMP MIB walk is performed in the interval between when SNMP is first enabled and the SNMP daemon is ready, the MIB walk action will return an error.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1030, what caveat is documented for Certificates?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Certificates - When a switch uses a certificate with a legacy certificate name that is not supported in 10.12 because it contains disallowed characters, the information will migrate properly in the upgrade, but that certificate can no longer be edited. For new certificate names, only alphanumeric characters, dots, dashes, and underscores are allowed.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1030, what issue was resolved in Config Mgmt Bug 313068?
original_answer: Config Mgmt (Bug ID 313068): Symptom: Static routes are deleted after an upgrade from AOS-CX 10.08.1010 to later versions. original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1030, what issue was resolved in Central Bug 300693?
original_answer: Central (Bug ID 300693): Symptom: Firmware updates via Aruba Central fails with an event log, Event | 4403 | LOG_ERR | CDTR | 1 | User admin: primary image update failed via HTTPS from common.cloud.hpe.com. Scenario: This issue is observed when the switch is configured to use http-proxy and the switch's upstream DNS server does not have an entry for common.cloud.hpe.com and when DNS recursion is disabled. Workaround: Configure a static DNS entry for common.cloud.hpe.com using the ip dns host command.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1030, what issue was resolved in MAC Authentication Bug 313116?
original_answer: MAC Authentication (Bug ID 313116): Symptom: Auth request flooding is seen due to continuous MAC moves when a dual uplink AP is connected to the switch. Scenario: This issue is observed when the dual uplink AP connected to switch ports sends LLDP frames on both the ports with concurrent onboarding and device profile enabled. Workaround: Disable concurrent onboarding and disable LLDP authentication, or remove the device profile and disable LLDP authentication.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1030, what issue was resolved in DHCP Snooping Bug 312815?
original_answer: DHCP Snooping (Bug ID 312815): Symptom: Clients are unable to receive the IP address when DHCP snooping is enabled and when the source MAC address is different from the client hardware address. Scenario: Ethernet MAC address and client hardware address are diffrent for the clients. This issue is observed when DHCPv4 snooping drops the DHCP offer / DHCP acknowledgment packets when the source MAC address differs from the client MAC address field in the DHCP discover / request packets. This issue is seen even when the verify MAC option is disabled. Workaround: Configure the client hardware address as static MAC address in the MAC table.
cleaned_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1030, what issue was resolved in DHCP Snooping Bug 312815?
cleaned_answer: DHCP Snooping (Bug ID 312815): Symptom: Clients are unable to receive an IP address when DHCP snooping is enabled and the source MAC address differs from the client hardware address. Scenario: Ethernet MAC address and client hardware address are different for clients. This issue is observed when DHCPv4 snooping drops DHCP offer/DHCP acknowledgment packets when the source MAC address differs from the client MAC address field in the DHCP discover/request packets. This issue is seen even when the verify MAC option is disabled. Workaround: Configure the client hardware address as a static MAC address in the MAC table.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1030, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1030, how do you restore configuration after a software downgrade?
original_answer: Downgrade Restore: To restore configuration after a software downgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, PL.10.xx.yyyy). 2. Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. 3. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1030, how do you perform the software upgrade?
original_answer: Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method. 2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue. 3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow unsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time. 4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.
cleaned_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1030, how do you perform the software upgrade?
cleaned_answer:
Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow these steps in order:
1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.
2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any device updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue.
3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow unsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1031, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1031, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1031, what caveat is documented for SNMP?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: SNMP - When SNMP is enabled via the switch CLI, it can take between 1-2 minutes for the SNMP daemon to be ready to respond to requests. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue. 3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow unsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time. 4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow unsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1040, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1040, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1040, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1040, how do you restore configuration after a software downgrade?
original_answer: Downgrade Restore: To restore configuration after a software downgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, PL.10.xx.yyyy). 2. Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. 3. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1040, how do you perform the software upgrade?
original_answer: Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method. 2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue. 3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow unsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time. 4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow these steps in order:
1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.
2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any device updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue.
3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow unsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1050, what caveat is documented for Client IP Tracker?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Client IP Tracker - User-Based Tunneling (UBT) and DHCPv4/v6 snooping are mutually exclusive features in AOS-CX. When these two features are configured simultaneously, binding entries for the end clients will not be populated in the binding database. IP source-lockdown feature permits traffic from the clients to pass through the switch only if the client's MAC and IP addresses are present in the binding database. IP source lockdown can be enabled at both port and VLAN levels. The port-level configuration will be overridden by the VLAN-level configuration only when the port is a member of a single VLAN. If the port is a member of multiple VLANs, then the VLAN-level configuration for IP source lockdown will not be applied. Client IP tracker can track the IP address until the IP source lockdown is applied. Once the lockdown is in effect, the IP address will only be tracked if there is a valid entry in the binding table. However, if an IP address is already being tracked, it will remain active through probes, as probes utilize ARP packets, which are Layer 2 (L2) packets rather than Layer 3 (L3) packets.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1050, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1050, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1050, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1050, how do you restore configuration after a software downgrade?
original_answer: Downgrade Restore: To restore configuration after a software downgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, PL.10.xx.yyyy). 2. Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. 3. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1050, how do you perform the software upgrade?
original_answer: Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method. 2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue. 3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1060, what caveat is documented for Client IP Tracker?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Client IP Tracker - User-Based Tunneling (UBT) and DHCPv4/v6 snooping are mutually exclusive features in AOS-CX. When these two features are configured simultaneously, binding entries for the end clients will not be populated in the binding database. IP source-lockdown feature permits traffic from the clients to pass through the switch only if the client's MAC and IP addresses are present in the binding database. IP source lockdown can be enabled at both port and VLAN levels. The port-level configuration will be overridden by the VLAN-level configuration only when the port is a member of a single VLAN. If the port is a member of multiple VLANs, then the VLAN-level configuration for IP source lockdown will not be applied. Client IP tracker can track the IP address until the IP source lockdown is applied. Once the lockdown is in effect, the IP address will only be tracked if there is a valid entry in the binding table. However, if an IP address is already being tracked, it will remain active through probes, as probes utilize ARP packets, which are Layer 2 (L2) packets rather than Layer 3 (L3) packets.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1060, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1060, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1060, how do you restore configuration after a software downgrade?
original_answer: Downgrade Restore: To restore configuration after a software downgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, PL.10.xx.yyyy). 2. Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. 3. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1060, how do you perform the software upgrade?
original_answer: Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method. 2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue. 3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow unsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time. 4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1070, what caveat is documented for Client IP Tracker?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Client IP Tracker - User-Based Tunneling (UBT) and DHCPv4/v6 snooping are mutually exclusive features in AOS-CX. When these two features are configured simultaneously, binding entries for the end clients will not be populated in the binding database. IP source-lockdown feature permits traffic from the clients to pass through the switch only if the client's MAC and IP addresses are present in the binding database. IP source lockdown can be enabled at both port and VLAN levels. The port-level configuration will be overridden by the VLAN-level configuration only when the port is a member of a single VLAN. If the port is a member of multiple VLANs, then the VLAN-level configuration for IP source lockdown will not be applied. Client IP tracker can track the IP address until the IP source lockdown is applied. Once the lockdown is in effect, the IP address will only be tracked if there is a valid entry in the binding table. However, if an IP address is already being tracked, it will remain active through probes, as probes utilize ARP packets, which are Layer 2 (L2) packets rather than Layer 3 (L3) packets.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1070, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1070, what issue was resolved in Port Access Bug 333709?
original_answer: Port Access (Bug ID 333709): Symptom: Interface remains down post a CoA port bounce, with interface details reason showing as Authorization Change. original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1070, what issue was resolved in 801.1X Bug 330413?
original_answer: 801.1X (Bug ID 330413): Symptom: The access-clients won't be authenticated successfully via 802.1x Auth. Scenario: If MAC-Authentication is successful first and MBV is applied for client, then 802.1x EAPOL packet sent by clients won't be processed by Port-Access daemon, as a result the 802.1x-Auth process won't be completed. Workaround: 802.1x Auth has to be triggered first before MAC-Auth gets triggered, which can be achieved by applying appropriate auth precedence & priority and disabling concurrent auth.
cleaned_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1070, for AOS-CX 6000 version 10.13.1070, what issue was resolved in 801.1X Bug 330413?
cleaned_answer: 801.1X (Bug ID 330413): Symptom: Access-clients fail to authenticate via 802.1x Auth. Scenario: If MAC-Authentication is successful first and MBV is applied for the client, then 802.1x EAPOL packets sent by clients are not processed by the Port-Access daemon, resulting in incomplete 802.1x-Auth process. Workaround: Trigger 802.1x Auth before MAC-Auth using appropriate auth precedence & priority and disabling concurrent auth.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1070, what issue was resolved in VLANS Bug 302131?
original_answer: VLANS (Bug ID 302131): Symptom: Not possible to set dot1qVlanStaticEgressPorts and dot1qVlanStaticUntaggedPorts MIB objects using a HEX value, only string was supported. original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1070, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1070, how do you restore configuration after a software downgrade?
original_answer: Downgrade Restore: To restore configuration after a software downgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, PL.10.xx.yyyy). 2. original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1070, how do you perform the software upgrade?
original_answer: Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method. 2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue. 3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow unsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time. 4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow these steps in order:
1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.
2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any device updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue.
3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow unsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1080, what caveat is documented for Central?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch is able to connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the Aruba Central inventory or does not have a proper license, the switch will get disconnected. If the Aruba Central feature is enabled using this command, the switch will then reconnect back to Aruba Central and will get disconnected again. This connect/disconnect process will continue until the switch is properly registered in Aruba Central. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, best practice is to disable Aruba Central until the switch is registered in Aruba Central, or a license is obtained for that device.
cleaned_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1080, what caveat is documented for Central?
cleaned_answer: Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to Aruba Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. To prevent this cycle, disable Aruba Central until registration or licensing occurs.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1080, what caveat is documented for Client IP Tracker?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Client IP Tracker - User-Based Tunneling (UBT) and DHCPv4/v6 snooping are mutually exclusive features in AOS-CX. When these two features are configured simultaneously, binding entries for the end clients will not be populated in the binding database. IP source-lockdown feature permits traffic from the clients to pass through the switch only if the client's MAC and IP addresses are present in the binding database. IP source lockdown can be enabled at both port and VLAN levels. The port-level configuration will be overridden by the VLAN-level configuration only when the port is a member of a single VLAN. If the port is a member of multiple VLANs, then the VLAN-level configuration for IP source lockdown will not be applied. Client IP tracker can track the IP address until the IP source lockdown is applied. Once the lockdown is in effect, the IP address will only be tracked if there is a valid entry in the binding table. However, if an IP address is already being tracked, it will remain active through probes, as probes utilize ARP packets, which are Layer 2 (L2) packets rather than Layer 3 (L3) packets.

IP source-lockdown feature permits traffic from the clients to pass through the switch only if the client's MAC and IP addresses are present in the binding database. If the port is a member of multiple VLANs, then the VLAN-level configuration for IP source lockdown will not be applied. original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1080, what issue was resolved in IGMP Bug 338204?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 338204): Symptom: An IPTV may experience a delay when changing channels. This problem may occur intermittently depending on the number of multicast entries. Scenario: The multicast entries present in the database need to be updated with the activity-status every 30 seconds. The activity-status need to be fetched from the hardware for all the multicast entries. This is taking 5-6 seconds of time in the customer network. So, the channel change, which is leading to the forwarding port change in the hardware, is pending behind the update of activity-status of multicast entries.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1080, under what scenario does IGMP Bug 338204 occur?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 338204): The multicast entries present in the database need to be updated with the activity-status every 30 seconds. The activity-status need to be fetched from the hardware for all the multicast entries. This is taking 5-6 seconds of time in the customer network. So, the channel change, which is leading to the forwarding port change in the hardware, is pending behind the update of activity-status of multicast entries.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1080, what known issue is documented for IGMP Bug 341085?
original_answer: IGMP (Bug ID 341085): Symptom: Multicast traffic is being blocked after upgrading from AOS-CX 10.12.0006 or earlier versions of AOS-CX. Scenario: If the switch has a control plane ACL configured AND has IGMP or PIM configured on a pre-10.12.1000 build and upgrades to 10.12.1000 or a later release, the implicit deny in the control plane ACL will block multicast traffic thus inhibiting functionality of those features. Workaround: Manually add an ACE to the control plane ACL to explicitly permit multicast traffic.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1080, how do you restore configuration after a software downgrade?
original_answer: Downgrade Restore: To restore configuration after a software downgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, PL.10.xx.yyyy). 2. Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. 3. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1080, how do you perform the software upgrade?
original_answer: Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method. 2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue. 3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow unsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time. 4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.
cleaned_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1080, how do you perform the software upgrade?
cleaned_answer:
Upgrade Procedure: To perform the software upgrade, follow these steps in order:
1. Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.
2. Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any device updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue.
3. When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow unsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
4. Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.

original_question: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.13.1090, what caveat is documented for Client IP Tracker?
original_answer: Feature Caveat: Client IP Tracker - User-Based Tunneling (UBT) and DHCPv4/v6 snooping are mutually exclusive features in AOS-CX. When these two features are configured simultaneously, binding entries for the end clients will not be populated in the binding database. IP source-lockdown feature permits traffic from the clients to pass through the switch only if the client's MAC and IP addresses are present in the binding database. IP source lockdown can be enabled at both port and VLAN levels. The port-level configuration will be overridden by the VLAN-level configuration only when the port is a member of a single VLAN. If the port is a member of multiple VLANs, then the VLAN-level configuration for IP source lockdown will not be applied. Client IP tracker can track the IP address until the IP source lockdown is applied. Once the lockdown is in effect, the IP address will only be tracked if there is a valid entry in the binding table. However, if an IP address is already being tracked, it will remain active through probes, as probes utilize ARP packets, which are Layer 2 (L2) packets rather than Layer 3 (L3) packets.

IP source-lockdown feature permits traffic from the clients to pass through the switch only if the client's MAC and IP addresses are present in the binding database.
